retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Clinical Significance -- Prolonged Wound Healing
retrieved_docs.content: Possible causes of prolonged wound healing include: Diabetes mellitus Rheumatoid arthritis Vascular or arterial insufficiency Zinc deficiency Low human growth hormone levels Older age Obesity Trauma Excess moisture Tissue hypoxia Medications (steroids, nonsteroidal antiinflammatories, chemotherapy) Alcoholism Tobacco abuse

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Healing Phases -- Other Issues -- Wound Healing Controversies and the Future
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal tissue has been shown to heal without scars, but its clinical role has yet to be determined. Transforming growth factor and several other cytokines all have a role in healing, but when to add or how much of the cytokine is needed for adequate healing is still being debated. Hyperbaric oxygen can promote healing, but the technique and success rate are not well established. Even though there are many anecdotal reports on honey and wound healing, controlled studies show that the benefits of honey are marginal at best. Finally, medications that can adversely affect healing include anticonvulsants, steroids, antibiotics, angiogenesis inhibitors, and NSAIDs. Drugs known to promote healing include insulin, vitamins, thyroid hormone, and iron. The new frontier for wound healing is the use of stem cells, but there are ethical issues regarding the widespread use of this technology. [7] [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Impaired Wound Healing -- Issues of Concern -- Factors Affecting Wound Healing
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco Abuse

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Wound Healing -- Clinical Significance -- Chronic Wound Formation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with immunosuppression are more susceptible to infections and have a diminished ability to fight infections, leading to delayed wound healing due to an extended inflammatory phase. Anti-inflammatory drugs, particularly during the first 3 days of healing; corticosteroids; immunosuppressants; and chemotherapy agents can alter wound healing. [6] Similarly, some comorbidities negatively influence wound healing, with diabetes mellitus being one of the most significant diseases associated with chronic wound formation. Poor perfusion, neuropathy, immunosuppression, slower collagen synthesis and accumulation, and inadequate angiogenesis often cause an increased risk of infection and delayed wound healing in patients with diabetes mellitus.

retrieved_docs.title: Impaired Wound Healing -- Issues of Concern -- Factors Affecting Wound Healing
retrieved_docs.content: Malnutrition

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Mechanism -- Proliferative Stage
retrieved_docs.content: During this stage of healing, re-epithelialization and the replacement of the clot by granulation tissue occurs simultaneously. This stage is highly cellular, with the primary cell types including fibroblasts, keratinocytes, and endothelial cells. Fibroblasts function to synthesize collagen and the extracellular matrix. The granulation tissue comprises an extracellular matrix, proteoglycans, hyaluronic acid, collagen, and elastin. Cytokines, growth factors, interleukins, and angiogenesis factors are active during this time and can continue anywhere from days to weeks under normal circumstances. Granulation tissue grows from the base of the wound and can typically fill any size of the wound. Any errors in granulation tissue formation can result in chronic wound formation. Foreign bodies at the site of a wound can also result in persistent granulation tissue and poor wound healing, with excess macrophages, fibroblasts, and capillaries reacting around the foreign material.

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Healing Phases -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the initial phases of wound healing. Identify the mediators of the inflammatory phase of wound healing. Outline the physiologic processes involved in the proliferative phase of wound healing. Summarize the factors that promote the development of chronic wounds. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Impaired Wound Healing -- Issues of Concern -- Factors Affecting Wound Healing
retrieved_docs.content: The WHP is very complex and involves high levels of coordination between multiple tissues and cell types [6] . Consequently, impairment in the process can occur at any step along the sequence that leads to process completion. Many known factors can affect or modulate wound healing [7] . In the subsequent sections, we will discuss major modulators (both positive and negative) of the WHP, including a summary of some of the methods and techniques devised to promote wound healing [9] . retrieved_docs.content: Negative Pressure Therapy

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Granulation tissue may persist under several circumstances resulting from underlying conditions or errors in the stages of wound healing (errors in the proliferative phase and remodeling phase). [1] [2] [3] [4] [6] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Physiology -- Mechanism -- Proliferation
retrieved_docs.content: The proliferative phase occurs 3 to 21 days after injury and involves processes of angiogenesis, granulation tissue production, collagen deposition, and epithelialization. The primary outcome of this phase is the filling of the wound defect. Hypoxic conditions in the wound bed lead to the synthesis of nitric oxide (NO) by endothelial cells which stimulate vascular endothelial growth factor to release and promotes angiogenesis. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Assessment -- Clinical Significance -- Chronic
retrieved_docs.content: Age and Hormones It is commonly recognized that, in healthy older adults, the effect of aging causes a temporal delay in wound healing but not an actual impairment in terms of healing quality A review of age-related changes in healing capacity demonstrated that every phase of healing undergoes characteristic age-related changes. Compared with females, aged males have been shown to exhibit delayed healing of acute wounds. Studies indicate that estrogen can improve the age-related impairment in healing in both men and women, while androgens negatively regulate cutaneous wound healing. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Healing Phases -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Wounds generally heal in 4 to 6 weeks. Chronic wounds are those that fail to heal within this timeframe.

retrieved_docs.title: Impaired Wound Healing -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe factors that are associated with better wound healing. Identify the wounds that are likely to experience impaired healing. Summarize the recent advances in wound care and treatments available. Explain how use of an interprofessional team leads to better outcomes in the care of wounds. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Impaired Wound Healing -- Issues of Concern -- Factors Affecting Wound Healing
retrieved_docs.content: Stress has been demonstrated to be a major contributor to a broad range of health conditions and illnesses, including cardiovascular disease, cancer, and obesity. Stress states lead to upregulation of stress hormones via the hypothalamic-pituitary axis and the release of adrenocortical hormones. Resultant changes include elevated levels of cortisol, glucocorticoids, and catecholamines. Cortisol works to blunt the immune response by blocking the production of important cytokines such as IL-1beta, IL-6, and TNF-alpha. The impairment of immune response ultimately leads to deficient wound healing [25] [26] .

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Assessment -- Clinical Significance -- Chronic
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyurea has been reported in multiple instances to cause non-healing ulcerations Chemotherapeutic drugs delay cell migration, decrease wound matrix formation, lower collagen production, impair fibroblast proliferation, and inhibit wound contraction; furthermore, they weaken immune functions, thereby impeding the natural inflammatory phase of healing, and increasing the risk of infection [15] Steroids decrease inflammation, inhibit epithelialization, and decrease collagen production [15] Vitamin A reverses delayed wound healing from steroids [39]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2052
retrieved_docs.content: ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are classified as a type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS. The broader category PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS includes CICATRICANTS, which are agents that promote scar formation and facilitate wound healing. Consequently, enzymes used in wound and ulcer therapy belong to the larger group of cicatricant preparations, highlighting their role within the spectrum of therapeutic agents aimed at promoting tissue repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1876
retrieved_docs.content: Preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as cicatrants, meaning they are agents that promote tissue repair and scar formation, and are used to enhance the healing process of wounds and ulcerative lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1408
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers, indicating that these enzymatic agents constitute a specific type of therapeutic preparation used to manage wound and ulcer conditions. This relationship highlights that enzymatic formulations belong to the broader category of wound‑and‑ulcer treatments, emphasizing their role within the spectrum of medical preparations aimed at promoting healing and tissue repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are classified as a specific type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS, indicating that they belong to the broader therapeutic category used to manage wounds and ulcers. Within this category, PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS also include cicatrizants, which are agents that promote scar formation, and enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers, highlighting the diverse mechanisms employed in wound care. Proteolytic enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are a distinct subclass of enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers; they are identified as proteolytic agents that belong to the trypsin family and are associated with kolagenasy (collagenase activity), underscoring their role in breaking down proteinaceous debris and remodeling collagen in the wound environment. This hierarchical organization links general wound‑healing preparations to specialized enzymatic agents, illustrating how specific proteolytic and collagenolytic actions are integrated into comprehensive wound‑treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1845
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with cesarean section, indicating that patients who have undergone a cesarean delivery may present with a scar of unspecified type, a factor that clinicians should consider when evaluating postoperative healing, potential complications, and future obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 449
retrieved_docs.content: Proteolytic enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as members of the trypsin, bromelain and collagenase enzyme families. As proteases, these agents act by breaking down proteinaceous debris and damaged tissue, thereby facilitating debridement and promoting wound healing. Their inclusion in the trypsin, bromelain and collagenase groups reflects shared biochemical properties that are exploited in clinical management of chronic wounds and ulcerative lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 418
retrieved_docs.content: ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are a specific type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS, placing enzyme‑based wound care products within the broader category of therapeutic agents used to manage wounds and ulcers. PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS themselves are classified under the larger group of cicatrizants, which are agents that promote tissue repair and healing. In addition, the category “enzymes for treatment of wound and ulcers” is also a sub‑type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS, highlighting that both the general enzyme formulation and the specific ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are encompassed by this therapeutic class. This hierarchical organization clarifies that enzyme‑based wound‑healing products are both a form of cicatrizant and a distinct subset of preparations designed for wound and ulcer management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 346
retrieved_docs.content: debridement and promote regeneration. Together, these relationships illustrate how antagonist and inhibitory mechanisms intersect with specific enzyme classifications, how a wide array of general anesthetic drugs is systematically categorized, and how wound‑healing proteases fit into the larger enzymatic hierarchy, providing clinicians with a comprehensive framework for selecting and understanding these therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2079
retrieved_docs.content: Proteolytic enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as members of the kolagenasy group, indicating that they function as collagenases that degrade collagen in wound tissue and thereby facilitate debridement and healing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1788
retrieved_docs.content: Proteolytic enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers constitute a therapeutic class of proteolytic agents that are employed to debride necrotic tissue and promote healing in chronic wounds and ulcerative lesions. These enzymes are classified as members of the trypsin family, share functional properties with bromelain, and exhibit collagenase activity. As specialized enzymes for the treatment of wounds and ulcers, they integrate the proteolytic mechanisms of trypsin, bromelain, and collagenase to facilitate effective wound care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 381
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers, indicating that they belong to the broader category of therapeutic agents used to promote healing of cutaneous injuries and ulcerations. Within this category, preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers also encompass cicatrizants, which are wound‑healing substances, and enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers, a sub‑group that specifically relies on enzymatic activity to aid tissue repair. Proteolytic enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are a distinct type of enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers; they are proteolytic agents that belong to the kolagenasy (collagenase) family. By degrading damaged collagen and other extracellular matrix components, these proteolytic enzymes facilitate debridement and accelerate the healing process in wounds and ulcers. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate a hierarchical framework in which enzyme‑based preparations, including proteolytic and collagenolytic agents, function as specialized cicatrizants within the overall therapeutic approach to wound and ulcer management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 631
retrieved_docs.content: Proteolytic enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are a specific class of enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers, indicating that they belong to the broader category of therapeutic wound‑debriding enzymes. They are also described as a member of the kolagenasy (collagenase) group, meaning that these proteolytic agents function as collagen‑breaking enzymes used to remove necrotic tissue and promote healing in chronic wounds and ulcers. This classification underscores their role as specialized proteolytic agents within wound‑care regimens, highlighting their importance for effective debridement and tissue repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 452
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as a specific type of preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers, and conversely, preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers encompass enzymes for treatment of wounds and ulcers. This hierarchical relationship indicates that enzyme‑based products constitute a subset within the broader category of wound‑and‑ulcer preparations, highlighting how therapeutic formulations for wounds and ulcers can be organized by their active components and guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate wound‑care interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN and High risk are both clinically associated with cesarean section, indicating that an unidentified type of cesarean scar and a high‑risk patient status are relevant factors in the consideration, planning, and postoperative management of cesarean deliveries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 198
retrieved_docs.content: dermatologic, hair‑care, and cosmetic products, underscoring its versatility in clinical nutrition, wound‑healing, and dermatologic formulations. The concurrent use of tryptophan as an inactive ingredient further illustrates the broader application of essential amino acids beyond their nutritional functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2386
retrieved_docs.content: جرح قطع (cut wound) is classified as a specific type of جرح مفتوح (open wound), indicating that cut wounds are a subset of open wounds and share the characteristic of tissue exposure that requires appropriate assessment and management.
